content.problemStatement:
You have two strings SSS and TTT of lengths NNN and MMM respectively.

In one operation, you can:


Either select a **proper** substring from any of the strings and delete it.

In this case, remaining parts of the string are concatenated in order.
Or, copy a **proper** substring from any of the strings and append the substring at the beginning or end of the other string.
Find the **minimum** number of operations needed to make both the strings equal.

It is guaranteed that the strings can be made equal using finite number of operations.

Note that a substring uuu is said to be a proper substring of string vvv if u≠vu \neq vu=v.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and MMM — the lengths of strings SSS and TTT respectively.
The second line of each test case contains two space-separated strings SSS and TTT.


Output Format
For each test case, print the **minimum** number of operations needed to make both the strings equal.


It is guaranteed that the strings can be made equal using finite number of operations.
constraints: SSS and TTT consist of lowercase english alphabets only.
The sum of NNN and MMM over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.input:
3
2 2
ab ab
3 2
akc ac
4 2
abcd bc
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Both strings are already same. Thus we require 000 operations.
Test case 222: Select substring S[2,2]S[2,2]S[2,2] from SSS and delete it. Thus both strings become equal in 111 operation.
Test case 333:

Select substring S[1,1]S[1,1]S[1,1] from SSS and delete it. Now, S=bcdS = bcdS=bcd.
Select substring S[3,3]S[3,3]S[3,3] from SSS and delete it. Now, S=bcS = bcS=bc.

Thus both strings become equal in 222 operations.

hidden.input:
3
2 2
ab ab
3 2
akc ac
4 2
abcd bc
